title: Distilling Large Language Models for Network Active Queue Management
authors: Shiva Raj Pokhrel, Deol Satish, Jonathan Kua, Anwar Walid
publish: IEEE Transactions on Networking
abstract: We propose AQM-LLM, a framework that distills Large Language Models for Active Queue Management in modern networks. Unlike conventional learning-based AQMs that require extensive feature engineering and struggle with dynamic conditions, our approach leverages the contextual reasoning capability of LLMs to enhance the Low Latency, Low Loss, and Scalable Throughput (L4S) architecture with minimal manual intervention. The L4S-LLM design introduces three key components: (i) a state encoder that transforms heterogeneous network telemetry into token embeddings, (ii) a specialized L4S-LLM head that produces congestion actions in a single inference step, and (iii) a data-driven Low-Rank Adaptation scheme that drastically reduces trainable parameters while preserving accuracy. Our open-source FreeBSD-14 implementation demonstrates improved queue delay stability and higher bandwidth utilization for both DCTCP and UDP Prague traffic. We emphasize that this work demonstrates architectural feasibility through controlled experiments; deployment on router-class hardware will require additional model optimization (e.g., compression, pruning, or quantization) and is left for future work.

title: PVSizer: An Open-Source Python Framework for PV and BESS Sizing and Impact Analysis in Distribution Networks
authors: Yayu Yang, Samuel N. Okhuegbe, Jian Zhang, Apsana Bhandari, Zhengfa Zhang, Haozong Wang, Muhammad Umar Afzaal, Yuru Wu, Junjie Yin, Fangxing Li, Yilu Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industry Applications
abstract: High photovoltaic (PV) penetration in distribution systems may cause voltage violations and thermal overloading, while existing open-source tools do not provide an integrated and automated approach for systematic PV and battery energy storage system (BESS) sizing under feeder constraints. To address this gap, this paper presents PVSizer, an open-source tool for PV-BESS sizing and impact assessment in distribution networks. Built on OpenDSS and a Python-based automation layer, PV Sizer provides three modules: (1) Impact Analysis/Time-Series Simulation for detailed quasi-static time-series (QSTS) analysis of a specified configuration; (2) Traversal for parallel feasible domain mapping under feeder operational constraints; and (3) Optimization based on constraint-guided incremental search (CGIS) to identify the maximum feasible PV capacity together with the minimum required BESS support. Case studies on a real utility feeder with over 700 nodes show strong site dependence in feasible PV-BESS sizing limits. The Optimization module reaches the same final solution as exhaustive Traversal with substantially lower computational burden, demonstrating the usefulness of PVSizer for feeder-constrained PV-BESS integration studies.

title: Rate-Distortion Trade-off in Integrated Communication, Sensing, and Control Closed-Loop Systems
authors: Yinglong Fan, Zhiyong Feng, Zeyang Meng, Zhiqing Wei, Ying Zhou, Kai Niu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Vehicular Technology
abstract: The rapid advancement of wireless communication technologies has accelerated the adoption of networked closed-loop control systems in intelligent manufacturing. Within this context, the integrated communication, sensing, and control (ICSC) framework has emerged as an important paradigm for modeling and analyzing large-scale real-time closed-loop systems. However, existing studies frequently overlook the impact of downlink communication on closed-loop control performance and fail to comprehensively model the interactions between communication, sensing, and control, making it difficult to identify the achievable performance limits under constrained communication resources. Therefore, this paper establishes a rigorous theoretical framework that explicitly accounts for both uplink sensing measurements and downlink control commands. Based on causal directed mutual information theory, we derive a lower bound on the downlink rate-distortion function that decomposes into a dynamics-dependent term and an innovation-dependent term. The results clearly quantify the influences of sensing quality and closed-loop dynamics on the minimum communication rate required to achieve a specified distortion level, and further clarify the connection between downlink communication constraints and achievable closed-loop control performance. The simulation results demonstrate the validity of the bound and further illustrate that the minimum downlink communication rate increases significantly with greater system dynamic complexity, higher sensing errors, and tighter distortion constraints. The findings reveal a fundamental rate-distortion trade-off in closed-loop systems, providing a theoretical foundation for the joint design of communication, sensing, and control.

title: Tournament-Based Index Obfuscation for Secure Space Shift Keying MIMO Systems
authors: Raed Mesleh, Mohammad Abudayah, Saud Althunibat
publish: IEEE Transactions on Vehicular Technology
abstract: A tournament-based index obfuscation framework is proposed and analyzed for space shift keying (SSK) multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) systems. Algebraic properties of Paley tournaments are exploited to construct deterministic yet nontrivial permutations of the transmit antenna indices. These permutations are refreshed on a per-channel basis through a channel-dependent cyclic shift derived from reciprocity. As a result, a matched index-domain channel is observed at the legitimate receiver, whereas an eavesdropper is forced to operate under a mismatched decoding metric. In contrast to conventional physical-layer security techniques, the proposed framework operates exclusively in the index domain and preserves the inherent energy-efficiency and low-complexity advantages of SSK. In practical SSK implementations where the number of transmit antennas is not a power of two, only a subset of valid antenna indices is used for information mapping, while the remaining antennas participate only in the obfuscation mechanism. Reliability and secrecy are characterized through average bit error rate, mutual information, generalized mutual information, secrecy rate, equivocation, and information leakage. Both passive and active adversarial scenarios are considered. Passive attacks include uninformed mismatched eavesdropping as well as partially correlated eavesdropping channels that emulate spatial proximity and common scattering. Active training-phase attacks, including pilot contamination and forced permutation manipulation, are also examined and mitigated through tournament-structure consistency checks leading to block acceptance/rejection. The proposed cyclic-shift realization is shown to operate effectively when the legitimate nodes observe consistent channel information, while its sensitivity to channel estimation mismatch is also discussed. Numerical results demonstrate that reliable communication is preserved at the legitimate receiver, while a substantial loss in the eavesdropper' achievable rate is induced even under correlated and active attack conditions. For benchmarking purposes, a comparison with artificial-noise based SSK is also included, highlighting the fundamental difference between power-domain and index-domain physical layer security mechanisms.

title: YOLO-SAE: Spatial-Adaptive Attention Enhancement for Small-Object Detection With Quantified Accuracy-Efficiency Trade-offs
authors: Syam Widiyanto, Hsiu-Ming Wu
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Small-object detection in aerial imagery remains challenging due to background clutter, occlusion, and extreme scale variation. This paper presents YOLO–Spatial Attention Enhancement (YOLO-SAE), an attention-augmented extension of YOLOv8 that integrates two complementary components: (i) a multi-head self-attention (MHSA) block inserted at the P5 stage to incorporate long-range contextual cues at coarse feature resolution, and (ii) a compact pre-head refinement module placed before the detection heads, instantiated as Efficient Channel Attention (ECA) or Shuffle Attention (SA) to recalibrate decision-time features. Under identical training protocols and single-scale evaluation at an input resolution of 640 on VisDrone 2019 and DOTA 2.0 (HBB), the most accuracy optimal configuration (MHSA+SA) improves mAP@0.5 from 0.299 to 0.405 and mAP@ [0.5:0.95] from 0.164 to 0.236 on VisDrone, and from 0.208 to 0.333 and from 0.109 to 0.190 on DOTA, respectively. To ensure computational transparency, we report parameters, GFLOPs, and runtime breakdown (pre-/inference/post-processing) for each variant, highlighting the accuracy–efficiency trade-offs of single and composite attention designs. All experiments were conducted Google Colab using Python 3 on an NVIDIA A100 GPU under the same environment for latency and throughput reporting. We emphasize that the individual attention components are established; our contribution is a deployment-oriented composition and placement strategy with quantified accuracy–efficiency trade-offs under a controlled and reproducible protocol.

title: SpineVLM: A Markdown-Guided Structured Fine-Tuning Framework for Spine X-ray Report Generation
authors: Dong Liu, Wenhui Li, Ning Xu, Guoge Han, Rui Hao, Xianzhu Liu, An-An Liu
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: Automated medical report generation in specialized fields like spine radiography is constrained by data scarcity and high annotation costs. Consequently, existing multimodal large language models (MLLMs) struggle in these settings, often missing minute, scattered spinal abnormalities. We introduce SpineVLM, a data-efficient framework for structured spine X-ray report generation. The framework is built upon the newly constructed SXRG dataset, comprising 10,468 image-report pairs developed via a hierarchical AI-assisted annotation pipeline. To optimize learning under limited data, we propose Markdown-Guided Structured Learning (MGSL), which reformulates unconstrained free-text synthesis into a structured completion task, acting as a strong regularizer. Furthermore, an unsupervised Region-Focused Inference (RFI) module powered by foundation models (DINOv2) isolates the vertebral column to enhance the perception of subtle lesions without requiring manual spatial annotations. Evaluated on a 7B-parameter vision-language backbone, SpineVLM achieves strong performance against ten baseline multimodal models across standard linguistic metrics. In a double-blind reader study, the system achieved a diagnostic F1-score of 0.866, comparable to specialist performance, while reducing clinical reporting time by over 41%. By open-sourcing the dataset and codebase, we provide, to our knowledge, the first quantitative benchmark for automated spine radiography report generation, together with a structured framework for this data-limited setting. All data and code will be publicly released at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/LiuDongDaniel/SpineVLM</uri>.

title: Energy Efficient Federated Learning Over Wireless Networks: A GNN-assisted Deep Reinforcement Learning Approach
authors: Liang Wang, Zihao Wei, Bomin Mao, Qu Luo, Qihao Peng, Pei Xiao
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: Implementing federated learning (FL) over wireless networks faces critical research challenges, such as high communication costs, inevitable communication latency, and significant energy consumption for model transmission and training, primarily caused by device heterogeneity and unpredictable dynamic channel conditions. This paper proposes Graph-based Resource Optimization with Compression for FL (GROC-FL), a unified framework that jointly coordinates wireless resource allocation and collaborative model compression. By leveraging Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) to model wireless topology and Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) to optimize communication and computation resources together with a globally consistent sparse update mechanism, GROC-FL minimizes the overall energy consumption of clients over wireless networks. In order to address the intrinsic topology dependence of wireless FL, we develop a graph-augmented DRL agent based on a graph convolutional network (GCN) that captures resource competition and network topology. We further develop a collaborative model compression module, termed Federated Parameter Negotiation (FPN), which enables clients to negotiate a global sparse mask and further reduce energy consumption during FL training. Experimental results demonstrate that GROC-FL outperforms the baselines in energy consumption, training performance, and client fairness.

title: Dual-Polarized Endfire Phased Array Antenna with Forward Beam Coverage for IoT Devices
authors: Yuepeng Yu, Changjiang Deng, Ruge Yao, Zilin Qiao
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: In this paper, a dual-polarized endfire phased array antenna (PAA) based on tilted-beam elements is presented to provide forward beam steering for IoT Devices. Each antenna element comprises two mirror-symmetric modules to generate ±45° polarizations, with their normal directions oriented to the endfire direction. Each module contains two dipoles with unequal lengths, excited by a 1-to-2 feeding network to achieve forward beam tilting. This achievement is helpful to avoid large grating lobes, even with large element spacing of 0.76λ in array design. To further suppress the cross-polarization level at the zenith, a gradient polarization metasurface (GPM) is placed tightly above the elements. It can provide additional polarization for the broadside beam, without influencing the endfire beam. A 1×6 dual-polarized PAA prototype operating in the 4.8 GHz band is fabricated. The measured beam steering range is from 0° to +70° with a 3.8-dB gain fluctuation, exhibiting forward and low-elevation coverage. Within this range, the measured side lobe level is below -7.9 dB, the cross-polarization level is below -9.7 dB, and dual polarizations are consistently maintained.

title: Dynamic Analysis and Vibration Reduction of HTS Maglev Vehicle Using MRD Semi-active Control
authors: Yuchen He, Xucheng Zhou, Haitao Li, Xianhong Hu, Mingxuan Yin, Zigang Deng, Gino D'Ovidio
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Vehicles
abstract: The limited intrinsic damping characteristics of the high-temperature superconducting (HTS) pinning maglev system pose challenges to effective vehicle vibration reduction under external disturbances such as permanent magnetic guideway (PMG) irregularities. This paper incorporates a magnetorheological damper (MRD) into the secondary suspension of HTS pinning maglev vehicles for semi-active vibration control. First, an MRD dynamic model considering the influence of current is presented, and a vehicle dynamic model developed based on an existing HTS pinning maglev test line and HTS-PMG relationship obtained from experiments. Subsequently, a semi-active vibration reduction co-simulation model is constructed using the skyhook (SH) linear continuous control strategy to evaluate the vertical and lateral dynamic performance of the car body under different operating speeds influenced by PMG irregularities. The results indicate that the semi-active damping strategy based on MRD ensures safe operation while effectively reducing carbody vibration, achieving over 50% vibration reduction rate at higher speeds, demonstrating its clear potential for improving the dynamic performance of HTS pinning maglev vehicles in practical engineering.

title: Design and Control of a Self-Balanced Quadruple Boost 17-Level Switched Capacitor-Based Inverter
authors: Abdul Nadeem, Yogesh V. Hote
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: A switched capacitor (SC) multilevel inverter produces high-quality, pseudo-sinusoidal stepped voltage waveforms with reduced total harmonic distortion (THD). It offers substantial voltage amplification capabilities, rendering it suitable for a variety of power conversion applications, including industrial systems and renewable energy integration. Multiple voltage levels are produced from a single DC source through the use of capacitors and switching mechanisms, thereby aiding in the reduction of overall system cost and size. A singlephase switched-capacitor (SC) inverter capable of producing seventeen voltage levels is introduced. The topology operates with one DC input source, eleven unidirectional switches, and two bidirectional switches, enabling a quadruple-boost output voltage. The proposed converter utilises parallel charging and series discharging techniques for capacitors, in conjunction with a DC voltage source, to achieve an output voltage four times the input voltage. A modulation strategy based on nearest-level control is adopted to keep the capacitor voltages balanced even when the load varies. Furthermore, the inverter is compared with existing MLI topologies, demonstrating its superior performance. The feasibility and effectiveness of this inverter are verified through MATLAB simulations under static resistive loads, inductive loads, dynamic load conditions, and different modulation index values.

title: Economic and Operational Analysis of Virtual Energy Storage in Energy Communities
authors: Vishal Sharma, Shubham Patwari, Satish Sharma, Prerna Jain
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: The recent decline in storage technology costs and the rise of economically-driven energy exchange mechanisms are fostering the growth of energy communities. This paper presents an evolved transactive control framework for shared energy communities featuring independent energy suppliers, retailers, and prosumers. The focus is on a virtual energy storage system (VESS), wherein each supplier has an individual battery. Due to underutilization and high costs, prosumers often cannot fully leverage their battery capacity. By aggregating suppliers' batteries, we create a virtual capacity that exceeds the actual physical capacity. This system optimizes battery usage since not all prosumers charge or discharge simultaneously. The control problem discussed involves the efficient scheduling of energy operations for a collection of prosumers, each with their own demand and generation capabilities, and a group of energy storage suppliers that can store and retrieve excess energy from the prosumers. This paper proposes a centralized system in which a central authority oversees and controls all energy transactions, enhancing the efficiency and sustainability of energy usage in these communities.

title: Cost Effective Reconfigurable Induction Motor Stator Windings for Single Phase Integrated Onboard Charger
authors: Deepak Sai Duvvapu, Barry Venugopal Reddy, Srinvas. K
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Accelerating electric vehicle (EV) battery charging while ensuring long-range remains a pivotal challenge in advancing sustainable transportation. An Integrated On-Board Charger (IOBC) emerges as a compelling solution by repurposing existing traction drive components as an AC-DC front-end converter during charging. This results in significant reductions in charger size, weight, and overall system cost leaving much space for the battery to increase drive range. However, a key challenge lies in managing uneven winding current distribution, which can induce torque ripple and compromise system performance. This paper presents a modified IOBC topology that effectively mitigates this issue, ensuring balanced current flow across motor windings and equal magnetic stress during the charging process. Notably, the proposed solution requires only minimal hardware reconfiguration, enabling seamless operation in both vehicle-to-grid (V2G) and grid-to-vehicle (G2V) modes. The electromagnetic performance of the proposed architecture is designed and validated through finite element analysis using ANSYS, while steady-state operation and control strategies are verified through detailed simulations in MATLAB/Simulink. The findings highlight the practical viability and efficiency of the proposed IOBC design for next-generation EV applications.

title: Hyperbolic Micro-Expression Recognition
authors: Zixi Chen, Boxuan Hu, Junlin Hu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Biometrics, Behavior, and Identity Science
abstract: Micro-expression recognition is a fine-grained classification task that aims to classify brief, involuntary facial expressions into some basic emotion categories. The primary challenge lies in extracting robust feature representations due to the short duration, low intensity, and significant individual variability of micro-expressions. In this paper, we study micro-expression recognition problem in hyperbolic space, and propose a hyperbolic micro-expression recognition (HypMER) method that exploits vision Transformer architecture and learns a deep distance metric in hyperbolic space. Our HypMER designs a hyperbolic mapping module to project Euclidean features into hyperbolic space, leveraging the unique geometric properties of hyperbolic space to better represent hierarchical data. Additionally, we combine a hyperbolic space-based loss and a Euclidean space-based loss to optimize feature learning by exploiting the strengths of both Euclidean and hyperbolic spaces. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmark datasets show that our HypMER outperforms state-of-the-art methods, achieving notable improvements in unweighted F1-score (UF1) and unweighted average recall (UAR) and verifying its effectiveness.

title: Information-Theoretic Sensor Control for Multiple Extended Target Tracking Using Adaptive Hölder Divergence
authors: Yating Liu, Guoqing Qi, Yinya Li, Andong Sheng
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: This article proposes an adaptive sensor control strategy within the GGIW-LMB filtering framework for elliptical extended target tracking, aiming to jointly optimize kinematic states, target extent, and cardinality. With improving sensor resolution, shape information (e.g., target size) has become increasingly important for tracking and decision-making. In practice, however, heavy clutter, missed detections, and time-varying target numbers significantly degrade the accuracy of target state and cardinality estimation. To address these challenges, a closed-form approximation of the Hölder divergence is derived specifically for the GGIW-LMB filtering framework, which enables efficient information-theoretic sensor control without particle-based numerical methods. Building on this result, an adaptive Hölder-divergencebased sensor control strategy is developed, in which the divergence parameter is dynamically adjusted according to uncertainties in target existence and state estimates. The proposed algorithm jointly accounts for kinematics and shape parameters of elliptical extended targets and improves robustness in challenging scenarios. Finally, the effectiveness of the proposed approach is validated through simulation results.

title: Embedded Realization of Deep Learning Based LMPC for PMSM Drives
authors: Akash Patil, Yoshit Tapar, Pramod Ubare, Nirlipta Ranjan Mohanty, D. N. Sonawane
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: In today's time, Model Predictive Control (MPC) has gained attention as a viable alternative to conventional control approaches in the motor control applications due to its capability to handle multi-input, multi-output constraints. However, due to its optimization-based nature, MPC requires significant computational resources, necessitating strong hardware and robust algorithms when implemented on embedded systems. MPC's computational efficiency can be improved by using better optimization solvers or better MPC design, but each has its own set of limitations. This research suggests deep neural network based linear model predictive control (DNN-MPC) as a method to reduce computational burden while maintaining performance characteristics. Additionally, this study demonstrates a systematic process of embedded implementation on low-end microcontrollers. DNN model is trained using data generated through a Monte Carlo method based on the LMPC model. The DNN-MPC attained an accuracy of 97.28%, acquiring all the characteristics of LMPC. Despite this negligible reduction in accuracy, the DNN-based model offers substantial improvements in computational efficiency, making it a viable alternative for real-time applications. The proposed DNN-MPC approach has validated using hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) co-simulation on a low-end STM-32 microcontroller. Furthermore, this study also presents a systematic approach of Embedded code-generation on embedded hardware.

title: Design, Analysis and Control of Multi-Mode Cell Voltage Equalizer Using Fly-Back Converter for Series-Connected Battery String
authors: Abhaya Kumar Sahoo, Keerthi Saladi, Sashwathkumar S, Lizanne Fernandes, V. Sankaranarayanan
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Conventional direct-cell-to-cell (DC2C) cell voltage equalizers exhibit lower balancing speed, particularly in large battery strings, due to only a single active balancing path at any given time. This sequential charge transfer from high to low voltage cells counterparts continues until voltage uniformity is achieved across all cells, resulting in extended balancing duration. To address the above limitation, this study proposes a multi-mode DC2C cell voltage equalizer designed to enhance balancing speed under various cell voltage imbalance conditions. The proposed architecture employs a single fly-back converter operating in three distinct modes, each tailored to specific imbalance scenarios to optimize energy redistribution. A comprehensive design methodology, operating principle, and control strategy for the multi-mode equalizer are presented, supported by flowchart. Simulation analysis justify that the proposed multimode DC2C equalizer achieves significantly improved voltage balancing performance compared to conventional single-path equalizers, thereby validating its efficacy and suitability for high-performance battery management systems.
